Now the next question is, well why is every sale the same? See when I said to them that night they were like, well what are you talking about? Every sale is not the same Jordan and in fact you're probably thinking the same thing right now. You're saying to yourself, you know, Jordan, you're totally wrong about that one. I see what you're saying here with the other stuff but every sale is different. Everyone has different needs, different objections, different beliefs, different values, different pain points and my answer to you is yeah, that's all completely true but it's not what I'm talking about. When I said every sale is the same what I meant that night and what turned out to be probably the most profound revelation I've ever had in my life especially when it comes to sales is that despite all those differences I just mentioned, people's needs, their objections, their values, their pain points, despite all that individual stuff, the same three key elements still have to line up in a prospect's mind before you have any shot of closing them and it doesn't matter what you're selling them or how you're selling it to them or how much it costs or how much money they have whether it's a tangible product or it's an intangible idea or a vision whether it's over the phone you're selling or in person doesn't matter. If in a single moment in time you can create these three crucial core elements in another person's mind then bam you have an excellent shot of closing them and conversely if even one of those three core elements is missing you basically have no shot at all of closing them. Here's what they are. We call these three crucial elements the three tenths and the context here is a scale of certainty in your prospect's mind that runs from one to ten with ten being the most certain someone could possibly be and a one being the least certain someone could possibly be. So again if a prospect is currently at a ten and it means he's in a state of absolute certainty at that moment and if he's currently at a one then he's in a state of absolute uncertainty at that moment. Now watch if a one represents complete uncertainty and a ten represents absolute certainty then here in the very beginning of the sale when a prospect first walks through your door and you haven't said even a word to them yet he's typically going to have some amount of uncertainty about your product. These are his preconceived notions and they're based on things like his gut reaction when he first laid eyes on your product perhaps he's done some research on the internet about your product he's looked at other similar products maybe he's got some preconceived notions about you as the salesperson and of course could be any one of those things or it could be all of them working together. In the beginning someone is typically very uncertain about things so they want to think about them and at the end of the sale the goal is to make them absolutely certain about your product so they want to buy it. Does that make sense? You see someone is very uncertain about things in the beginning so they want to think about them that's only natural then we help them become absolutely certain that what we're offering is the best fit for them so that they want to buy it. You see people don't buy when they're uncertain that's the way it should be. So what we're doing here what's happening as you're moving someone down the straight line you're moving them from a state of uncertainty to a state of absolute certainty that's your goal. Now remember there are actually two types of certainty you have certainty on a logical level which we call a logical certainty then we have certainty on an emotional level which we call emotional certainty and here's the deal people do not buy on logic they buy on emotion then they justify with logic but when you satisfy the logic center of the brain first it then opens them up to be moved emotionally one without the other doesn't work very well.
